topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

The postcard, glossy and aggressively cheerful, lay on the coffee table like a landmine. Sarah traced the outline of the distant mountains with a trembling finger. Liam had packed up his life, his entire universe, and was heading to Seattle. The silence in the apartment was suddenly deafening, amplifying the frantic thumping in Sarah’s chest. She kept picking at a loose thread on the rug, her gaze darting toward the front door as if expecting a sudden interruption. Every mundane sound—the distant siren, the hum of the refrigerator—felt magnified, sharp and unwelcome. She needed to move, to do something, anything, to stop the swirling anxiety that made her hands slick with sweat.

[story 2]

"So, you're actually leaving?" Maya asked, her voice unnervingly bright, as she stared at the itinerary spread across the kitchen counter. Ben didn't look up from polishing a seldom-used mug, his movements stiff and precise. Every time Maya mentioned the packing deadlines, Ben would stop his polishing, his shoulders tightening almost imperceptibly. He started pacing the small space between the stove and the counter, his footsteps quick and uneven. He kept rubbing the back of his neck, a repetitive gesture that betrayed the sudden tension coiling in his stomach. He wanted to ask a thousand questions, but the words caught in his throat, leaving only a dry, rasping sound.

[story 3]

I stared at the text message again. *Moving.* It was a single word, yet it felt like a seismic event. My jaw was clenched so tightly I could feel the muscles protesting beneath my skin. I walked to the window, pulling the curtains aside just enough to see the street below, watching the cars rush by with an almost aggressive speed. My breath hitched, shallow and fast. I kept re-reading the confirmation email, the words blurring into meaningless shapes. A coldness seeped into my limbs, making my fingers numb. I needed a distraction, something solid to focus on, because the sheer weight of the impending change was pressing down on my chest, making every inhale a deliberate, strained effort.
